title: Sets, Relations and Functions
subject: Mathematics
content:
# Sets, Relations and Functions

## Quick Synopsis

This chapter defines mathematical objects and mappings. JEE uses it in inverse functions, domain-range, composition, and counting relation types.

## Must Know

- Set operations include union, intersection, difference, complement, and Cartesian product.
- Relations are subsets of Cartesian product.
- Reflexive, symmetric, transitive relations define equivalence relations.
- Functions require every input to have exactly one output.
- One-one, onto, bijective, composite, and inverse functions are central.
- Domain restrictions are critical for logarithmic, radical, rational, and trigonometric functions.

## Formulas / Rules

- n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∩ B).
- Number of relations from A to B = 2^(mn) if |A| = m and |B| = n.
- Number of functions from A to B = n^m.
- If f is bijective, f inverse exists.
- (f ∘ g)(x) = f(g(x)).

## JEE Focus

- Find domain before simplifying expressions.
- For inverse, check one-one nature and range.
- Use graphs for greatest integer, fractional part, modulus, and signum functions.
- Composition order matters.

## Common Traps

- Every function is a relation, but every relation is not a function.
- Inverse relation may exist but inverse function needs bijection.
- Squaring both sides can introduce extraneous roots.
- Range after composition may be smaller than apparent codomain.

## 10-Minute Revision Checklist

- Can you find domain and range?
- Can you test one-one and onto?
- Can you form inverse functions?
- Can you count relations/functions?
